sections.heading: God, Man, and Creation
sections.html:
The Purpose of Human Existence

The Baltimore Catechism opens with the question that governs all that follows: the reason for human existence. The answer given is not primarily about moral duty or religious observance but about relationship — a relationship of knowledge, love, and service that culminates in eternal happiness with God.

Q. Why did God make you?
A. God made me to know Him, to love Him, and to serve Him in this world, and to be happy with Him forever in the next.

This foundational question echoes the teaching of Scripture throughout. The Psalmist declares that the LORD is our portion and our inheritance (Ps 16:5), and that in God's presence is fullness of joy (Ps 16:11). Christ himself summarizes the law in terms of the total orientation of the person toward God: love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength (Mk 12:30). The catechism is thus structured around the conviction that human beings are restless until they rest in God — a truth echoed by Augustine and embedded in the deposit of faith.

Q. What must we do to gain the happiness of heaven?
A. To gain the happiness of heaven we must know, love, and serve God in this world.

Scripture reinforces this: John 17:3 — "And this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent." Knowledge of God is not merely intellectual assent but the intimate, covenantal knowing that Israel experienced with YHWH.

The Nature of God and the Holy Trinity

The catechism defines God as the supreme, all-perfect Spirit who always was, always will be, and always will be the same. He is infinite in knowledge, power, and goodness. From this definition the catechism moves immediately to the Trinity, since the Christian God is not merely a philosophical Supreme Being but the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit revealed in Scripture.

Q. Is there but one God?
A. Yes, there is but one God.
Q. How many Persons are there in God?
A. In God there are three Divine Persons — the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost.

The unity of God is asserted in the Shema (Deut 6:4): "Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one." Yet the New Testament reveals within this unity a distinction of persons. At the baptism of Jesus all three persons are present simultaneously: the Son in the water, the Father's voice from heaven, and the Spirit descending as a dove (Matt 3:16–17). Christ commissions his disciples to baptize "in the name" — singular — "of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" (Matt 28:19), indicating three persons sharing one divine name.

  • John 10:30 — "I and the Father are one."
  • John 15:26 — The Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father.
  • 2 Cor 13:14 — The apostolic blessing invoking all three persons.

Creation, Angels, and Original Sin

God created the world and everything in it from nothing. Among his creatures he made the angels — pure spirits of great intelligence and power — and he made man, body and soul, in his own image and likeness (Gen 1:26–27). The catechism teaches that God endowed our first parents, Adam and Eve, with sanctifying grace, with freedom from concupiscence, and with immortality — gifts that were not owed to human nature but were given freely.

Q. What is original sin?
A. Original sin is the sin Adam committed, the consequences of which we all inherit — namely, loss of sanctifying grace, darkening of the intellect, weakening of the will, and an inclination to evil called concupiscence.

The fall of the angels preceded the fall of man. Some angels, led by pride, refused to serve God and were cast into hell (Rev 12:7–9; Jude 6). Adam and Eve, tempted by the devil, disobeyed God's command, and through their sin death entered the world and spread to all men (Rom 5:12). Paul draws the stark contrast: "For as by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous" (Rom 5:19). This is the drama of fall and redemption that the rest of the catechism unfolds.

  • Gen 3:1–19 — The narrative of the fall.
  • Rom 5:12–21 — Paul's Adam-Christ typology.
  • Ps 51:5 — "In sin did my mother conceive me" — the universality of original sin acknowledged.

sections.heading: Jesus Christ and Redemption
sections.html:
The Incarnation and the Two Natures

The second great movement of the catechism concerns Jesus Christ, true God and true man. The eternal Son of God, the second person of the Trinity, took to himself a human nature — body and soul — in the womb of the Virgin Mary. This union of two natures in one divine Person is the Incarnation, the central mystery of the Christian faith.

Q. Who is Jesus Christ?
A. Jesus Christ is the Son of God, the second Person of the Blessed Trinity, true God and true man.
Q. Why did the Son of God become man?
A. The Son of God became man to redeem us from sin and to teach us the way to heaven.

John's prologue provides the scriptural foundation: "The Word was God... and the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:1, John 1:14). The prophet Isaiah had foretold this: "Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel" (Isa 7:14), which Matthew interprets as fulfilled in the birth of Jesus (Matt 1:22–23). The angel Gabriel announced to Mary that the child to be born of her would be called holy — the Son of God (Luke 1:35).

The catechism insists on the integrity of both natures. Christ was not merely a godly man nor a divine being who appeared human; he possessed a true human intellect and will, suffered genuine physical pain, and died a real death. At the same time he remained God, possessing all the divine attributes. The Council of Chalcedon (451) defined this mystery: one person in two natures, without confusion, without change, without division, without separation.

The Passion, Atonement, and the Role of Mary

The catechism expounds the articles of the Apostles' Creed relating to Christ's suffering, death, descent, and resurrection. Jesus Christ suffered under Pontius Pilate, was crucified, died, and was buried. These are not mere historical events but the means of our redemption. Christ's death on the cross is a sacrifice of infinite value, making satisfaction for the sins of the whole world.

Q. What did Jesus Christ suffer?
A. Jesus Christ suffered a bloody sweat, a cruel scourging, was crowned with thorns, and was crucified.
Q. Why did Christ die on the cross?
A. Christ died on the cross to atone for our sins and to open heaven to us.

Paul's summary is decisive: "For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God" (2 Cor 5:21). Isaiah had prophesied the vicarious suffering of the Servant: "He was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace" (Isa 53:5).

The catechism also honors Mary as the Mother of God (Theotokos). Because Jesus is truly God, the one who bore him in her womb is rightly called the Mother of God. Mary's Immaculate Conception — her preservation from original sin in anticipation of her Son's merits — and her perpetual virginity are taught as part of Catholic tradition. Her role as intercessor is modeled on her intervention at Cana (John 2:1–11), where her request moved Christ to perform his first sign.

  • Luke 1:42 — Elizabeth's greeting: "Blessed are you among women."
  • Luke 1:48 — "All generations will call me blessed."
  • Rev 12:1 — The woman clothed with the sun, a type of Mary and the Church.

The Resurrection and Ascension

On the third day Christ rose bodily from the dead, conquering sin and death. The catechism treats the Resurrection not merely as a proof of Christ's divinity but as the ground of our own hope for resurrection. Christ descended into hell — that is, into the realm of the dead (Sheol/Hades) — to announce his victory to those who had died in faith awaiting redemption.

Q. What do we mean when we say Christ rose from the dead?
A. When we say Christ rose from the dead, we mean that on the third day his soul was reunited with his body, and he rose gloriously from the tomb.

Paul anchors Christian faith entirely in the bodily resurrection: "If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins" (1 Cor 15:17). The empty tomb, the appearances to Mary Magdalene (John 20:11–18), to the disciples on the Emmaus road (Luke 24:13–35), and to more than five hundred brethren at one time (1 Cor 15:6) — all establish the historical reality of the Resurrection.

Forty days after the Resurrection, Christ ascended bodily into heaven and sits at the right hand of the Father, interceding for us (Heb 7:25). He will come again in glory to judge the living and the dead. The catechism exhorts believers to live in readiness for this final coming, as Christ himself taught in the parable of the ten virgins (Matt 25:1–13).

sections.heading: Grace, Sacraments, and the Last Things
sections.html:
Sanctifying Grace and the Seven Sacraments

Grace is the supernatural gift by which God makes the soul pleasing to himself and fit for eternal life. Sanctifying grace is the habitual gift that makes the soul holy; actual grace is the transient divine help that moves the will to act rightly. The principal means by which Christ bestows sanctifying grace on the members of his Body are the seven sacraments.

Q. What is a sacrament?
A. A sacrament is an outward sign instituted by Christ to give grace.

The seven sacraments are: Baptism (water and the Trinitarian formula; removes original sin and infuses new life — John 3:5); Confirmation (strengthens the baptized with the gifts of the Holy Spirit — Acts 8:17); Eucharist (the body, blood, soul, and divinity of Christ under the forms of bread and wine — John 6:51); Penance (absolution from sins committed after baptism through contrition, confession, and satisfaction — John 20:23); Anointing of the Sick (healing of soul and sometimes body for those gravely ill — Jas 5:14–15); Holy Orders (ordination to the priesthood and diaconate — 1 Tim 4:14); and Matrimony (the sacramental bond of husband and wife — Eph 5:31–32).

  • Matt 28:19 — The institution of Baptism.
  • Luke 22:19–20 — The institution of the Eucharist at the Last Supper.
  • Matt 16:19 — The authority to bind and loose, the root of Penance.

The Commandments of the Church

Beyond the Ten Commandments of the Old Law, which Christ fulfilled and interiorized in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:17), the Church imposes several precepts on her members to ensure the minimum of spiritual and sacramental practice. These are the commandments of the Church: to assist at Mass on Sundays and holy days of obligation; to fast and abstain on appointed days; to confess one's sins at least once a year; to receive Holy Communion during the Easter season; and to contribute to the support of the Church.

The catechism also summarizes the seven capital sins (pride, covetousness, lust, anger, gluttony, envy, and sloth) and their opposing virtues. These are called capital because they are the source and root of other sins. Paul's exhortation to put on the armor of God (Eph 6:13–17) and his catalogues of sins and virtues in Galatians (Gal 5:19–23) form the scriptural backdrop for this moral instruction.

Death, Judgment, and the Last Things

The catechism concludes with the four last things: death, judgment, heaven, and hell. Every human soul will face a particular judgment immediately at death, and all will face the general judgment at the end of time, when Christ returns in glory.

Q. What are the four last things?
A. The four last things are Death, Judgment, Hell, and Heaven.

Heaven is the state of perfect happiness in the direct vision of God enjoyed by those who die in his grace. "No eye has seen, nor ear heard, nor the heart of man imagined, what God has prepared for those who love him" (1 Cor 2:9). Purgatory is a state of purification for those who die in God's grace but with temporal punishment for forgiven sins still to be discharged — grounded in the tradition of praying for the dead (2 Macc 12:46) and the Pauline teaching on the testing of works by fire (1 Cor 3:15). Hell is the eternal exclusion from God experienced by those who die in mortal sin without repentance — a reality Christ himself described in the most solemn terms (Matt 25:41; Mark 9:47–48).

  • Heb 9:27 — "It is appointed for man to die once, and after that comes judgment."
  • Rev 20:12 — The dead judged according to what was written in the books.
  • Matt 25:31–46 — The parable of the sheep and goats.
